nativeRange.habitat: Seaward coral-rich reefs exposed to currents; typically seen in small loose groups at moderate depths.

diet.notes: In the wild, the Pink Tail Trigger feeds largely on algae and detritus, supplemented by crustaceans, octopus, sponges, and small fish. In captivity, a varied diet works best: meaty foods such as krill, mysis, squid, clams, and chopped shrimp alongside marine algae or seaweed sheets. Including hard-shelled prey items like clams or crabs on occasion helps wear down the fish's continuously growing teeth.

imageCandidates.notes: Bernard DUPONT, 2765x1720. Crisp full lateral profile showing all diagnostic features: dark green body, yellow face/snout, green pectoral fin, blue-white dorsal/anal fins, and the signature pink caudal fin with white base.
Diego Delso, 4246x2831. High-res full-body shot; yellow face, yellow cheek fin, white black-edged fins and pink-white tail all clearly visible on a vibrant live individual.
Rickard Zerpe, 3241x2431. Dynamic in-situ pose with the erect blue first dorsal spine raised; shows yellow cheek marking and pink tail. Slight blue depth cast but sharp and colourful.

summary: The Pink Tail Trigger (Melichthys vidua) is an Indo-Pacific triggerfish recognized by its dark green body, yellow face, and bright pink tail. It is considered one of the more reef-tolerant triggerfish, though it still poses a risk to ornamental invertebrates and small fish.
careNotes: The Pink Tail Trigger is considered one of the more reef-tolerant triggerfish species — many keepers report that well-fed individuals leave most corals undisturbed — but it will still pose a risk to ornamental shrimp, small crabs, snails, and small fish, as tends to be the case with triggerfish generally. A minimum aquarium of around 700 L (approximately 185 US gallons) is generally advised, with ample open swimming space and a sturdy rockscape. Rocks should be placed directly on the bottom glass rather than on sand, as this species has been known to rearrange decor; larger systems are strongly preferred for long-term housing. Water parameters should track standard reef values: around 23–27 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, specific gravity 1.020–1.026. Handle with care: triggerfish have strong jaws and sharp teeth and are capable of delivering painful bites when threatened. Juveniles show distinctive dark radiating lines from the eye and along the fins that fade as the fish matures.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Husbandry parameters: 180 gal minimum, 72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, aggressive, reef-safe with caution, carnivorous diet of meaty foods plus hard-shelled items.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed family Balistidae, order Tetraodontiformes, max length 40 cm TL, depth 0-60 m, preferred temperature 24.6-28.9 C, Indo-Pacific distribution, omnivorous (algae/detritus + crustaceans/octopus/sponges/fish).

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Cites 2000 L recommended minimum (much larger than LiveAquaria's 680 L); rated highly aggressive; reef-safe-with-luck. Averaged with primary, biased toward larger per playbook tank-size rule and settled at 700 L.
